1: Jerubbaal (that is, Gideon) — the narrator supplies both names at the chapter opening. Yārubba'al ("Let Baal contend") is the name Joash coined after Gideon tore down the Baal altar (Judges 6:32): it declares that Baal has no power to avenge himself. The spring of Harod — ʿēn Ḥarōd — means "spring of trembling," possibly foreshadowing the fear test that follows.
2: "The people with you are too many for me to give Midian into their hands, lest Israel boast" — the Hebrew verb is yitpā'ēr (from pā'ar: boast, glorify). YHWH's stated concern is theological: if Israel wins by force of numbers, the narrative credit shifts from YHWH to human strength. This principle — that divine power is displayed through human weakness — is structurally identical to Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 1:27–29 that God chose the foolish and weak "so that no human being might boast before God."
3: "Whoever is fearful and trembling, let him turn back" — this applies the military exemption of Deuteronomy 20:8 ("Is there any man who is fearful and fainthearted? Let him go back to his house"). The departure of 22,000 of the 32,000 (68%) is not a failure of faith but obedience to Torah. The remaining 10,000 are still, as YHWH says (v4), "too many."
4: "I will test them for you there" — the Hebrew ʾeṣrᵊpennū lᵊkā uses the verb ṣārap (refine, test — as metal is tested by fire; cf. Psalms 12:6; Judges 66:10). YHWH performs a refining, not merely a selection. The water test is a divine winnowing of the army down to the divinely purposed number.
5: "Set apart everyone who laps…like a dog" — the verb lāqaq (to lap) is onomatopoeic and appears only here and v6 in the Hebrew Bible. Two postures: lapping with the tongue cupped by the hand (maintaining visual alertness) vs. kneeling on both knees (vulnerable, head down). The 300 lappers are not the more vigilant group — the point is simply that YHWH chose an arbitrary-looking minority to emphasize that the victory is his.
6: Three hundred men…cupping water with their hands — the exact figure: 300 out of 10,000 (3%). The absurdity is deliberate: the ratio makes any natural military explanation impossible. The Hebrew mᵊlaqᵊqîm bᵊyādām ʾel-pîhem (lapping with their hand to their mouth) reinforces that the hand, not the mouth, is the active agent — fitting for a victory where human hands will be mere instruments.
7: "With the three hundred men who lapped I will save you" — wᵊhôšaʿtî ʾetkhem: I will save you (the verb is yāšaʿ, the same root as Joshua/Jesus). YHWH is the subject; Gideon and the three hundred are instruments. The divine economy of weakness operates here: the fewer the human resources, the more visible the divine agency.
8: The three hundred took the provisions and trumpets — šôpārôt (ram's horns) become the primary weapon of the battle plan. The word for provisions is ṣēdāh (what one takes for a journey; food supply). Gideon sends 9,700 home while retaining 300 — the ratio of Judges 32:1. The camp of Midian lay below him "in the valley" — topographic advantage noted as the stage is set.
9: "Arise, go down against the camp, for I have given it into your hand" — divine prophetic perfect: the victory is stated as already accomplished (nᵊtattî, I have given). The nighttime oracle follows the pattern of Jacob at night (Gen 28), Moses at the burning bush, and later the vision accounts — YHWH breaking into human consciousness at night to commission a mission.
10: "But if you are afraid to go down" — the conditional acknowledges the possibility of fear. YHWH does not demand bravado; he accommodates weakness. The servant Purah (Pūrāh, possibly "branch" or "fruitful bough") is mentioned only here and v11 — an incidental detail that marks the account as historically specific.
11: "Your hands will be strengthened" — wᵊḥāzᵊqū yādêkā (and your hands will be strong). The idiom "strengthened hands" appears repeatedly in OT military contexts (Ezekiel 22:14; 2 Samuel 2:7) as the standard expression for courage and capability. YHWH offers intelligence before requiring action: "hear what they say, and afterward go down."
12: "Like locusts in abundance…as numerous as the sand" — two standard OT hyperboles for uncountable numbers. ḥāgāb lārōb (locusts in multitude) is the same image the prophets use for invading armies (Joel 1:4; Nahum 3:15). The deliberate contrast: 300 Israelites vs. an innumerable host. The narrator sets up the miracle by making the numerical disparity overwhelming.
13: "A loaf of barley bread came tumbling into the camp of Midian" — ṣᵊlîl lechem śᵊʿōrîm: a rounded (perhaps stale or heavy) loaf of barley bread. Barley (śᵊʿōrîm) was the food of the poor — wheat was preferred; barley was animal fodder or the food of those who could not afford better (cf. John 6:9, the five barley loaves). The dream symbolizes a despised, humble thing overthrowing what seemed indestructible.
14: "This is nothing other than the sword of Gideon the son of Joash" — a Midianite soldier interprets his own side's nightmare. The irony is striking: YHWH uses the enemy's dream to confirm his own commission to Gideon. ḥereb Gidʿōn (the sword of Gideon) — the Midianite interpreter has already collapsed the victory into a single name. "God has given into his hand" uses the divine name Elohim, not YHWH — appropriate in the mouth of a Gentile.
15: He worshiped. And he returned — wayyishtaḥū is the standard verb for full prostration in worship (the same verb used for Abraham, Moses, Joshua before YHWH or his messenger). Before giving any orders, Gideon worships. The sequence — receive the divine oracle, worship first, act second — is the pattern of biblical faith. His announcement to the troops ("Arise, for YHWH has given the host of Midian into your hand") echoes the divine word of v9 almost verbatim.
16: Three companies; a trumpet in every man's hand, along with empty jars, with torches inside — šôpārôt (ram's horn trumpets) + kaddîm (jars/pitchers) + lappidîm (torches/flames). The tactical logic: three simultaneous blasts from three directions will sound like a much larger army; smashing 300 jars simultaneously releases sudden blazing light in the dark. The torches are hidden inside the jars until the critical moment.
17: "Watch me and do likewise" — ûrᵊ'îtem kᵊ'ăšer ʾănî ʿōśeh wᵊken ta'ăśū: a verbatim command to imitate. The judge acts first and his men follow — the pattern of exemplary leadership. The specific command structure (Gideon acts, three companies follow simultaneously) requires extraordinary coordination for a pre-signal era battle.
18: "For the LORD and for Gideon!" — laYHWH ûlᵊGidʿōn: the battle cry pairs the divine name with the judge's name. This double invocation is unusual; in most holy war texts YHWH alone is invoked. Gideon's name alongside YHWH's reflects his role as YHWH's designated agent — but the order matters: YHWH first, Gideon second.
19: "At the beginning of the middle watch" — the middle watch (second of three; ca. 10 p.m.–2 a.m.) was when the first watch guards had just been relieved and the camp was most disoriented. The timing is tactically precise. wᵊhāqēm hēqîmū ʾet-haššōmᵊrîm — they had just posted the guards: the moment of maximum disorganization in a rotating watch system.
20: Torches in their left hands, trumpets in their right hands to blow — the tactical arrangement means no Israelite hand holds a sword: the weapons are light and noise. The cry ḥereb laYHWH ûlᵊGidʿōn ("A sword for the LORD and for Gideon!") declares a sword they do not wield — YHWH is the swordsman.
21: "All the host ran; they cried out and fled" — wayyārāṣ kol-hammaḥăneh wayyāriʿū wayyānūsū: the three verbs describe progressive disintegration — ran, cried out, fled. "Every man stood in his place" (ʾîš taḥtāyw, each man under him) — the 300 Israelites hold their positions while the enemy self-destructs. The contrast is between Israelite stillness and Midianite panic.
22: "YHWH set every man's sword against his fellow" — wayyāśem YHWH ʾēt ḥereb ʾîš bᵊrēʿēhū: YHWH is the grammatical subject; the panic-induced friendly fire is a direct divine act. The same pattern appears at Jehoshaphat's battle (2 Chronicles 20:23) and anticipates YHWH's eschatological wars (Zechariah 14:13). The flight route (Beth-shittah → Zererah → Abel-meholah → Tabbath) traces a real geography of retreat.
23: The men of Israel were called out from Naphtali, Asher, and all Manasseh — those who were dismissed in v3 are now summoned to pursue the fleeing enemy. The 300 who fought are joined by the many in the mopping-up phase: the decisive blow required few; the complete victory requires the many. This double-phase appears again in Judges 8:1–3 when Ephraim complains about being left out of the main battle.
24: "Come down against the Midianites and hold the waters" — lipdū ʾet-hammayim (seize the waters): cutting off retreat by controlling the water crossings, a standard ANE military tactic. Ephraim's role in the pursuit (capturing Oreb and Zeeb, v25) is theologically significant — the tribe that would later complain (Judges 8:1) was actually given the decisive honor of capturing the princes.
25: Oreb ("Raven") and Zeeb ("Wolf") — the names carry symbolic weight: predatory animals as names for enemy leaders. Their execution locations — ṣūr Ōrēb (Rock of Oreb) and yeqeb Zēʾēb (Winepress of Zeeb) — become permanent landmarks referenced in Psalms 83:11 and Isaiah 10:26, where they become the paradigm of YHWH's defeat of Israel's enemies. The heads brought to Gideon "beyond the Jordan" confirm the completion of the rout.
